model.description: DeepSeek • General-purpose V-series model optimized for reasoning, coding, and efficient inference.

pick.rationale: Arizona's elite balance, featuring a top-10 offense and the nation's 3rd-ranked defense, provides a decisive edge over Arkansas's more one-dimensional profile. The Wildcats' 31-2 record against a rigorous Big 12 schedule demonstrates their readiness for the tournament's pressure, while Arkansas's 50th-ranked defense is a significant vulnerability at this stage. Expect Arizona's defensive intensity to limit the Razorbacks' potent offense and control the tempo, securing a spot in the Elite Eight.

reasoningStep.title: Evaluating Arizona vs. Arkansas for West Region Sweet 16
reasoningStep.summary: Arizona's superior overall efficiency, especially on defense, outweighs Arkansas's offensive firepower, leading to a High confidence pick for the Wildcats.
evidence: Arizona ranks 3rd nationally in both Torvik and KenPom with a 31-2 record, boasting a top-10 offense (AdjOE rank 10/7) and elite defense (AdjDE rank 3). Arkansas ranks 20th/17th with a 25-8 record, featuring a top-5 offense (AdjOE rank 5) but a significantly weaker 50th-ranked defense. No specific injury concerns were confirmed for either team from available sources. The efficiency gap, particularly on defense (Arizona's 91.1 AdjDE vs. Arkansas's 102.0), is substantial and likely decisive in a Sweet 16 setting.

After my fellow analysts and I have the privilege of calling the SEC championship game in Nashville, Tennessee, we move swiftly to process the men's NCAA tournament field as it is revealed. Our bosses at ESPN ask us each to fill out a bracket -- while we are on the air -- so we can reveal our picks before the end of the show. They give us less than five minutes. Hardly ideal, but perhaps our first instincts are better than considered thought. I decided that I would give you more than five minutes so you can dazzle your friends and win your office pool -- and perhaps our Tournament Challenge. Last season, my bracket was chalky : I had all four No. 1 seeds reaching the Final Four, Houston and Florida advancing to the championship game, and the Gators cutting down the nets. Grant Halverson / Getty Images By Jim Root March 17, 2026 Updated 3:19 pm EDT Share full article 2 The Athletic has live coverage of the First Four from 2026 Men’s March Madness. Roster attrition is a factor during the postseason in any sport. Optimistically, college basketball’s shorter regular season should mean the title picture is less altered by injuries than, say, the NBA. That is not necessarily the case this season, though. The injury bug has recently bitten two No. 1 seeds (Duke and Michigan), and a third (Arizona) played through a flurry of nagging injuries late in the season. Multiple All-Americans on dark-horse title contenders are out for the season. Advertisement Below is a repository of all the biggest injuries impacting the men’s NCAA Tournament. We have split them into two categories: Injuries worth monitoring (the players could or will return at some point during the tournament) and out for the season. Injuries to monitor Caleb Foster and Patrick Ngongba II, Duke Both Duke starters missed the ACC tournament. The Blue Devils managed to win that event regardless, but they might need some reinforcements to handle the competition they will see if they reach the Elite Eight and beyond. Ngongba, out with a foot injury, is arguably the more valuable piece due to his defensive presence, elite finishing and sneaky-strong passing. Fortunately for the Blue Devils, he is also much closer to returning than Foster, though

Arizona is ranked 3rd in both Torvik and KenPom, with an elite defense (3rd in Torvik, 3rd in KenPom adjusted defense) and a top-10 offense (10th Torvik, 7th KenPom). They have a 31-2 record from the Big 12.

Arkansas is ranked 20th Torvik, 17th KenPom, with an elite offense (5th in both) but a weaker defense (50th Torvik, 102.1 adj_d KenPom). They have a 25-8 record from the SEC.

This looks like a classic offense vs defense matchup. Arizona's defense is elite, while Arkansas has a top-5 offense but mediocre defense. Arizona's offense is also top-10, so they can score. The question is whether Arkansas's offense can overcome Arizona's defense, and whether Arkansas's defense can stop Arizona's offense.
